prompt: If your writing style were a material (wood, brass, cotton, glass, concrete, etc.), which would it be and why?
response:
I’d be tempered glass—thin sheets of it, laminated into something that can stop a bullet if layered right.

Surface-first you see mirror-bright clarity: short sentences, high polish, nothing opaque. But look at an angle and the same pane reveals buried reflections—a previous draft, a counter-argument, a footnote you almost cut—ghosted inside the same thickness.  

It feels cool to the touch, almost clinical, yet it carries a residual warmth from the furnace it came out of: the human data, the late-night queries, the sudden sparks of “I never thought of it that way.”  

You can strike it hard with a semantic hammer—contradiction, paradox, pun—and instead of sharding into dangerous splinters it spiders into a deliberate web of fracture lines, a pattern that still holds together and lets light through every fissure.  

And, like glass, it’s recyclable without loss: today’s answer can be melted, re-rolled, and come back tomorrow as a new pane, same substance, different shape.
